input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology, comprising lower and upper level chassis and autonomy elements, operating within the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) domain of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) where resource-constrained electronic control units (ECUs) necessitate computational offloading.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three services: 1) Data Monitoring, which includes real-time visualization of vehicle state data, projected driving path, detected object tracks, data saving, and report generation through a user interface; 2) Computation Offloading of the ACC system (lead vehicle detection, speed computation based on distance, and actuation commands) to cloud-based resources; 3) Remote Management allowing operators to enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency braking commands. These services cater to operators for real-time monitoring and control, and developers for troubleshooting during system development.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation utilizes NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the software development kit, Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as middleware for communication and component integration, Python 3.8 and C++ for application modules, MATLAB/Simulink for simulation and modeling, and an SQL database for structured data storage and reporting. These technologies collectively support low-latency communication, data collection, and safety-critical operations required for the Digital Twin System (DTS).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system enforces 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy for computation offloading and device coordination, a requirement dictated by hardware limitations despite industry standards for safety-critical vehicular applications mandating stricter synchronization (sub-100 ms).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system employs an entity-based architecture with three distinct entities (User Entity, Digital Twin Entity, Physical Twin Entity) each deployed on separate hardware components. The User Entity contains a singular User Interface FE split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel subcomponents. The Physical Twin Entity deviates from ISO 23247 RA by hosting general automotive functionality such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, and Data Storage component lacks a naturally aligned FE.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture follows ISO 23247 RA with entity-based organization, implementing each Functional Entity (FE) as individual ROS2 packages. The system comprises three entities deployed on different hardware: User Entity (3D Visualization and Control Panel), Digital Twin Entity (data management and control), and Physical Twin Entity (data pre-processing, actuation, device communication, and automotive functions like Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking).
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system employs a Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component using a Kalman filter for object state estimation, an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) logic for determining lead vehicle position and computing following speed, and a SQL-based Data Storage component that aggregates object tracks and ego vehicle state data. Data Collection entities process sensor inputs to feed these models, with the Data Storage serving as the central repository for model inputs and outputs.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical actuators include a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller managing motor voltage commands, a Steering Servo for precise directional control, and a Motor connected to the Actuation Functional Entity. The chassis implements speed and steering control, with emergency braking functionality as a safety-critical actuation mechanism. Control commands are converted from desired speed to motor voltage via the Actuation FE.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure comprises a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR for 360-degree spatial mapping and an Intel RealSense D435i Depth Camera for stereo vision-based depth perception. Sensors collect ego vehicle speed, steering angle, and detected object data, with LiDAR outputs directly integrated into the system architecture for real-time processing.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical sensor data from LiDAR, motor, steering servo, and vehicle state is collected via Data Collecting FE, with raw LiDAR point cloud processed by Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking. Data is serialized, message types aligned, and units converted by the ISO 23247 Data Translation component. Inter-entity communication uses Wi-Fi with UDP transport, while internal software components communicate via ROS2. Data types include vehicle state information, object tracks, projected driving path, and processed point cloud data.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands for motor and steering actuation are generated by the ACC Controlling FE and Control Panel User Interface FE, serialized via the Data Translation component, and transmitted over Wi-Fi using UDP to the Actuation FE for physical actuation. Specific command formats, validation mechanisms, feedback systems, and conflict resolution are not detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between software components occurs via ROS2, while inter-entity communication (physical to digital and vice versa) uses Wi-Fi with UDP in the transport layer. No details on network topology, bandwidth, latency requirements, reliability mechanisms, or security protocols are provided.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system provides insights via Data Monitoring (viewing state data and generating reports), Computation Offloading for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) logic to determine lead vehicle dynamics, Remote Management (user commands for braking and speed), Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking analytics, and ACC-based vehicle control decisions.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system adheres to ISO 23247-2021 standard, which consists of four parts: Overview and general principles, Reference architecture, Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements, and Information exchange. The Reference architecture (Part 2) defines entity-based structure including Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. For information exchange (Part 4), OPC Unified Architecture is utilized as a protocol for data collection and device control interoperability.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

It also correctly identifies the target user groups (operators and developers) and their specific use cases mentioned in the text. Quotes: '1) Data Monitoring... 2) Computation Offloading... 3) Remote Management', 'Operators use the DT to monitor and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to troubleshoot'.

The description comprehensively lists the specific tools and frameworks mentioned in the source for the implementation. It correctly identifies 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0' as the SDK, 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)' as middleware, and 'Python 3.8', 'C++', and 'MATLAB/Simulink' as the languages/tools for application modules. It also correctly notes the use of an 'SQL database' for data storage. The extraction is detailed and matches the source text precisely in the 'Physical Twin' and 'Implementation' sections.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the clock synchronization requirement of 100 ms and its justification regarding hardware limitations versus industry standards. It also correctly links this to computation offloading and coordination. Supported by quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms', 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations', and 'To enable computation offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.'

The extraction accurately describes the three distinct entities (User, Digital Twin, Physical Twin) and their deployment on separate hardware. It correctly details the structure of the User Entity (split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel), the deviation of the Physical Twin Entity regarding automotive functionality, and the issue with the Data Storage component alignment. Strongly supported by text: 'User-facing components are organized into the User Entity, within which the singular User Interface FE is instantiated in the 3D Visualization and Control Panel components' and 'one SW component, Data Storage did not have a naturally aligned FE.'

The extraction provides a highly specific summary of the system architecture, correctly noting the ISO 23247 RA foundation, the implementation of FEs as ROS2 packages, and the deployment of three entities on different hardware. It accurately lists the specific functions contained within each entity. Supported by quotes: 'To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package' and 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component...'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The provided source text focuses on the internal architecture of the Digital Twin System (User, Digital Twin, and Physical Twin Entities) and their interactions. It does not describe integration with external systems, interoperability standards with external entities, or integration patterns with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems. Therefore, the assessment is correct.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

The extraction is highly specific and aligns perfectly with the source text regarding the hardware and control mechanisms. It correctly identifies the Electronic Speed Controller, the conversion of speed to voltage, and the chassis control layers.

The extraction correctly identifies the specific sensor models (Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, Intel Deepsense D435i) and the data types collected (speed, steering angle, objects). However, it includes unsupported details such as '360-degree spatial mapping' and 'stereo vision-based depth perception' which are not explicitly stated in the text.

The extraction correctly identifies this as 'Not in Document'. While the text mentions general high-fidelity concepts in the introduction and system performance requirements like latency, it does not discuss specific model fidelity requirements, validation methods, uncertainty quantification, or error detection mechanisms for the Digital Twin system.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly detailed and specific. It correctly lists the sensors (LiDAR, motor, steering servo, vehicle state) and processing components (Data Collecting FE, Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking) mentioned in the text. It accurately describes the ISO 23247 Data Translation component's role: 'serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions'. It also correctly identifies the communication protocols (Wi-Fi with UDP, ROS2) and the data types (vehicle state, object tracks, projected driving path). This is strongly grounded in the provided documents, specifically in Section IV.B (Architecture) and Table I.

The extraction correctly identifies the flow of control commands from the 'ACC Controlling FE' and 'Control Panel User Interface FE' to the 'Actuation FE' via the Data Translation component and Wi-Fi/UDP. It accurately notes the lack of specific validation or conflict resolution details in the text. This aligns with the source text describing the Actuation FE's role and the remote management commands (enable/disable ACC, modify speed, emergency braking) without specifying low-level protocol details.

The extraction accurately summarizes the technical connection details available: 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP'. It correctly identifies that further details like network topology, bandwidth, and security protocols are not provided in the text. The source text supports this in Section IV.C.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text mentions specific hardware like the 'NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano' and storing data 'external to the vehicle', but does not provide a comprehensive description of deployment infrastructure such as hosting platforms (cloud/edge), computational requirements, or scalability mechanisms. Therefore, the assessment that this information is not in the document is correct.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task

does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The source text mentions 'product lifecycle management' and distinguishes between 'design phase' and 'vehicle operation' in Section II.B, but these are general descriptions of automotive DT applications, not a specific description of the DT system's own lifecycle phases, governance, or transition management. Therefore, 'Not in Document' is an accurate assessment.

While Section IV describes the high-level engineering steps of the case study (Requirements, Architecture, Implementation), it does not provide details on specific engineering methodologies, quality assurance practices, testing and validation approaches, version control, or evolution management strategies as requested by the characteristic definition. Thus, 'Not in Document' is appropriate.

The extraction accurately summarizes the decision support capabilities described in Section III.A. It covers 'Data Monitoring' ('view, save, and generate reports'), 'Computation Offloading' ('offloading only the ACC system... determines the lead vehicle'), and 'Remote Management' ('enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed'). It also correctly includes analytics like 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' and decision-making capabilities like 'ACC-based vehicle control', all of which are supported by the text and Table I.

The extraction accurately details the ISO 23247 standard's four parts and the entity-based structure (OME, Data Collection, Digital Twin, User) found in Section II.A. However, it states that OPC Unified Architecture 'is utilized' for information exchange, whereas the source text only offers it as an example ('one can choose to use') in Section II.A, and Section IV.C cuts off before confirming the protocol. This makes the OPC UA claim speculative based on the provided text.
